title: ASX hits the skids as costs up 21pc
full_text:
ASX shares plunged 13 per cent on Tuesday after the market operator flagged that its costs would surge up to 21 per cent next year, driven by higher technology charges and moves to shore up its risk framework after a damning regulatory probe.
The update comes weeks after the Australian Securities and Investments Commission released findings from its inquiry into the exchange operator that found it had long prioritised shareholder returns over the need for a stable, secure and resilient market infrastructure.
In a market update on Tuesday, ASX said it expected total expenses growth guidance of between 18 per cent and 21 per cent in fiscal 2027, with operating expense growth, excluding depreciation and amortisation, of between 13 per cent and 16 per cent. The exchange operator also said it expected higher capital expenditure of between $180m and $200m in the 2027 calendar year, primarily driven by technology cost inflation and new product development.
For fiscal 2028, it sees capital expenditure between $170m and $190m. It also told investors its dividend policy remained unchanged: at a payout ratio of between 75 per cent and 85 per cent of underlying net profit after tax.
ASX also said it had entered into an agreement to sell its 49 per cent interest in property settlement play Sympli to its joint venture partner, ATI Group, for a nominal amount.
Investors punished the company following the announcement, pushing its shares down more than 13 per cent to $51.03.
The update follows hefty criticism from ASIC, after it found the sharemarket operator had an immature risk culture, compliance failures and poor supervision of listed companies.
In its final report into the ASX, released in April, ASIC raised concerns, and not for the first time, that ASX had for too long prioritised shareholder returns above all else. The regulator kicked off the probe into the ASX after a litany of trading outages and technology failures, including its abortive attempt to replace its ageing clearing and settlement technology CHESS.
ASIC hit the ASX with a $150m penalty in December, panning it for failing to balance the need to deliver investment returns with the obligation to reinvest in technology, processes and strategy to future-proof the business.
ASX heads to trial with ASIC in mid June. ASIC commenced proceedings in the Federal Court against ASX in 2024 for allegedly making misleading statements related to its CHESS project.
ASIC alleged statements made in ASX announcements in 2022 that the project remained on-track and was “progressing well” were misleading. ASX has filed its defence in the proceeding.
